EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Puerto Rico on the evening of Saturday 15 January, 2000 through early Sunday morning.  A very cool and dry airmass, unusual for these latitudes, established itself behind the front and persisted for about five days.  The coolest temperatures were observed during the period of January 16 to January 20.  Coastal temperatures were only in the mid 70s during the day and around 60 at night and early morning.  Inland sectors reported temperatures in the low 70s and mid 60s during the day and in the mid 40s and low 50s at night and early morning.  This represents about 5 to 10 degrees cooler than normal for this time of the year.

A strong cold front moved across Puerto Rico during the evening of Saturday 15, 2000. Strong winds followed this front especially during the early morning hours of Sunday. A spotter at Rincon in the western part of Puerto Rico reported a maximum wind gust of 70 mph. Another spotter in Juana Diaz indicated a maximum wind gust of  60 mph.  These winds downed several trees limbs.

A strong cold front, unusual for these latitudes, moved across Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands on January 15.  This front caused very strong winds and high seas.  The most affected areas were along the north coast of Puerto Rico with heavy surf conditions. The towns of Isabela and Catano sufffered minor coastal flooding where some coastal roads were partially covered by sand and water.

A Civil Defense official reported a a tornado along the Caguas expressway.
A Civil Defense official reported several funnel clouds along the Caguas expressway.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail over Caguas.
Heavy rains caused street flooding in Naguabo.

An man made fire on March 14, near the Maricao Reserve Forest in San German, went out of control  and expanded  to cover near 300 acres. Drier weather conditions and strong gusty winds helped the fire to spread out.  200 acres were located in the Maricao Reserve Forest and 100 acres were private. The fire last until the morning of March 18.  To extinct the fire 68,000 gallons of waters were used.

During the month of March average rainfall for Puerto Rico was 1.28 inches. The normal rainfall islandwide for March is 2.59 inches.   This represent a 50% less from the normal rainfall.  The areas most affected were the north and the northern interior sections of the island.  Rainfall deficiency over the southern sections of Puerto Rico was slightly below normal.

A strong and nearly stationary low pressure system north of the region produced large north swells and breaking waves greater than 10 feet. The areas most affected were the north coast of the islands. In Vega Baja three scuba divers and a swimmer resulted with injuries when they were smashed against the rocks by the rough seas.

A line of thunderstorms produced pea size hail over the municipalities of Gurabo and Cayey in central Puerto Rico.
A line of thunderstorms produced pea size hail over the municipalities of Gurabo and Cayey in central Puerto Rico.
A pilot flying at 1500 feet reported a funnel cloud just north of Vieques Island.

Dry weather has reduced flow of rivers in the northeastern part of the island.  About 4000 families in the high-lying areas  in the municipalities of Rio Grande, Luquillo and Fajardo has been left without water during peak usage hours in the mornings and early evenings.
A young person drowned at Jobos' Beach while practicing the sport of surfing.
Emergency Managers in Carolina reported landslides and urban flooding due to heavy rains.
Puerto Rico Emergency Managers in Guanica reported near three inches of rain in less than two hours .  This rainfall caused flooding problems on several roads and in a few houses.
Puerto Rico Emergency Managers indicated flooding in Luz del Cielo sector in Fajardo, over road 131 in Naguabo and on road 31 also in Naguabo due to a small stream out of its banks.
Emergency Managers Agency in Salinas reported a creek out of its banks and very heavy rains.  In addition a bridge in Guayama was destroyed by the flooding.
A few roads between Ponce and Juana Diaz and between Juana Diaz and Coamo were impassable due to heavy  rains.
Emergency Managers in Ponce reported flooding of a river channel.  In addition, a radio station indicated about four feet of water over some roads in Ponce.
A pilot reported two waterspouts 20 miles west of Aguadilla airport moving west southwest.

An upper level trough in combination with daytime heating produced very heavy rains across most of Puerto Rico.  This rainfall caused flash flooding, landslides, many impassable roads and half a dozen families in shelters in Catano. Some rainfall  amount reports were Yabucoa 7.48 inches, Guaynabo 4.61 and San Juan 1.81 inches.

Heavy rain across the San Juan metropolitan area caused a water rush in Puerto Nuevo river.  Four children between 6 to 11 years old were  were swept away by the waters and later rescued by police personnel.  Four policemen and three children suffered minor injuries.
A strong thunderstorm with very heavy rain produced between two to three inches of rain over the town of Guanica.  This rainfall caused flooding of streets and low lying areas.
Control Tower reported a waterspout four miles east of the San Juan Airport.

A middle to upper level trough combined with daytime heating produced scattered strong thunderstorms with very heavy rains. Various rivers in San German, Mayaguez and Adjuntas went out of their banks flooding adjacent areas. In Moca a  landslide was reported.
A pilot reported two waterspouts five miles west of Culebra moving west.
A weather service employee reported street flooding and nearly two inches of rain in Rio Grande.
Heavy rains flooded a house in Guaynabo due to poor drainage.
A weather service employee sighted two waterspouts between the island of Palomino just east of Fajardo and Vieques moving southwest  near 15 knots.
A local resident reported small hail over San German.
Navy personnel of Roosevelt Road Base sighted a funnel cloud that touched down on base briefly before dissipating.
A spotter reported winds of 40 mph and downed tree branches due to a strong thunderstorm.  In addition, Emergency Managers indicated flooding at Ponderosa sector due to heavy rain.
A T. V. Meteorologist reported about four inches of rain in Hato Rey sector causing Puerto Nuevo River to flood its banks.

A strong tropical wave moved across Puerto Rico during the evening hours affecting the south and southeast sections of  Puerto Rico with very heavy rain, flash flooding, mud and landslides. Reports indicated between 6 to 12 inches of rain during the evening and night hours. More than 100 houses were affected, especially in Salinas and Ponce, by flood waters and mudslides. A residence in Ponce collapsed and a child was slightly injured. This rainfall also forced several families into refugee centers.  Many roads became impassable due to landslides and flooding, and a bridge in Guayama collapsed.  Most of the rivers and streams went out of their banks.

A severe thunderstorm produced nickel size hail and strong winds that knocked down tree branches in Morovis and Manati.
A severe thunderstorm produced nickel size hail and strong winds that knocked down tree branches in Morovis and Manati.
A local resident reported a small stream over a bridge along road 146.

Debby formed on the 18th of  August as a tropical depression about 1100 miles east of the Windward Islands.  The storm moved west  northwest and strengthened to a hurricane on the morning of August 22 just northeast of the Leeward Islands. Debby passed about  30 miles north of St.Thomas late that morning and about  30 to 40 miles off the northeast coast of Puerto Rico during the afternoon.  The storm continued to move west northwest  and weakened during most of the day and night and by the 23rd it was located about 100 miles northwest of Puerto Rico as a tropical storm.   Its major impact over Puerto Rico was flooding. Some sectors in the interior received more than 12 inches of rain in a 48-hour period.  This caused widespread flooding and many rivers to overflow  their banks forcing communities to be evacuated.  Also this rainfall caused landslides and mudslides and induced various bridges to collapse. Many homes were affected by flood waters island wide.   Maximum sustained wind speed at the San Juan Airport was 24 mph with gusts to 35 mph. In Ceiba, maximum sustained winds were 28 mph with gust to 35 mph.  Damages were estimated around .5 M due to flooding affecting about 406 homes.  Caguas municipality was the most affected with 237 families reporting some degree of  flood damage.

Its major impact over Puerto Rico was flooding. Some sectors in the interior received more than 12 inches of rain in a 48-hour period.  This caused widespread flooding and many rivers to overflow  their banks forcing communities to be evacuated.  Also this rainfall caused landslides and mudslides and induced various bridges to collapse. Many homes were affected by flood waters island wide.   Maximum sustained wind speed at the San Juan Airport was 24 mph with gusts to 35 mph. In Ceiba, maximum sustained winds were 28 mph with gust to 35 mph.  Damages were estimated around .5 M due to flooding affecting about 406 homes.  Caguas municipality was the most affected with 237 families reporting some degree of  flood damage.

Debby formed on the 18th of  August as a tropical depression about 1100 miles east of the Windward Islands.  The storm moved west  northwest and strengthened to a hurricane on the morning of August 22 just northeast of the Leeward Islands. Debby passed about  30 miles north of St.Thomas late that morning and about  30 to 40 miles off the northeast coast of Puerto Rico during the afternoon.  The storm continued to move west northwest  and weakened during most of the day and night and by the 23rd it was located about 100 miles northwest of Puerto Rico as a tropical storm.   Its major impact over Puerto Rico was flooding. Some sectors in the interior received more than 12 inches of rain in a 48-hour period.  This caused widespread flooding and many rivers to overflow  their banks forcing communities to be evacuated.  Also this rainfall caused landslides and mudslides and induced various bridges to collapse. Many homes were affected by flood waters island wide.   Maximum sustained wind speed at the San Juan Airport was 24 mph with gusts to 35 mph. In Ceiba, maximum sustained winds were 28 mph with gust to 35 mph.  Damages were estimated around .5 M due to flooding affecting about 406 homes.  Caguas municipality was the most affected with 237 families reporting some degree of  flood damage.
Navy Personnel from Roosevelt Roads Base observed a waterspout moving inland into a housing area on base. No damage was reported.
A Private Meteorologist reported a funnel cloud near Mayaguez.
Flight Service Station reported a waterspout  about 9 miles west of Culebra.
Flight Service Station reported a  pilot sighting a waterspout  just east of  Roosevelt Roads Naval Base in Ceiba.
Heavy rain caused a river in Corozal to overflow its banks flooding adjacent areas. Landslides were also reported.
Local residents in the Joyuda sector sighted a waterspout  off shore.

A frontal trough just north of the region combined with daytime heating caused very heavy rainfall across the central and northeast sections of Puerto Rico. Several reports of flooding and landslides  were received from the municipalities of Comerio and Barranquitas with various residences and roads affected by the waters.  This rainfall then moved north affecting northeast Puerto Rico. The municipalities of Bayamon, San Juan, Guaynabo and Rio Grande were most affected with several homes affected by the waters and landslides.

A Weather Service Employee observed a  funnel cloud over the San Jose lagoon in San Juan.  It only lasted for about five minutes.

This rainfall then moved north affecting northeast Puerto Rico. The municipalities of Bayamon, San Juan, Guaynabo and Rio Grande were most affected with several homes affected by the waters and landslides.

Heavy rain caused several streams in Barranquitas to overflow their banks.  A car was swept away by the waters. Its occupant was rescued unharmed.  In Palo Incado sector various residences were affected by the waters.
A small stream went out of its banks in Naguabo flooding a nearby road.
A small stream went over a bridge in road 572 in Juana Diaz.
A radio station indicated that residents in Moca and San Sebastian reported small hail in association with a strong thunderstorm.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail near one inch in diameter in San Sebastian.
Heavy rains caused landslides between Lares and Utuado.

A strong tropical wave produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the eastern sections of Puerto Rico.  Strong gusty winds accompanied these showers. Wind damage was reported in Naranjito where aluminum tents wrapped onto power lines, in Corozal where winds knocked down power lines and trees and a residence lost its zinc roof and in  Vega Alta where a tree fell on a car injuring one person in the vehicle.  Other  wind damage were reported in Fajardo and Barranquitas.  In addition , two mudslides were reported on road 686 in Vega Baja.
